Event: On December 17, Fairchild made his first attempt at plant introduction by taking citron cuttings from Corsica. This was without proper authorization and was arrested for it.

While studying termites in Java, Fairchild discovers mangosteens, which he would later describe as the queen of tropical fruits; while he pushed for their incorporation on the american dieat throught his life, the fruit didn't really catch on.

While not first introduced by Fairchild into the US, he did ship some eucalytpus as well as other dry adapted species. On Fairchild's Tropical Botanic Garden there is an incredible specimen of a Rainbow eucalyptus (Eucalyptus deglupta)
Fairchild and Lathrop collect chayote (Sicyos edulis) and mangoes as some of their earliest accesions.
Fairchild ships more than 10 boxes of Chilean avocado seeds which would lay the foundation for the avocado fields in the American Southwest.

Crop: Bella sombra tree (Phytolacca dioica); Tipu tree (Tipuana tipu)
Event: Fairchild collected seeds of fast-growing shade trees later used in US plantings. In particular, bella sombra in California.
While it didn't have a big impact in the American economy, Fairchild shipped Itamaraca mango, a dwarf variety that is still found in backyards.
Fava beans were shipped for US trial
Fairchild sends Mediterranean vegetables including broccoli and peppers
First shippment of sultana grapes
Fairchild arrives in Egypt and sends cotton, sesame, garbanzo, and berseem for US testing
Collects pomelo from Ceram/Sekar during 40-day island cruise
Collects Carabao mango (later marketed as champagne mango)
Collects rice and water chestnut and builds China crop contacts
Bohemian hops arrive in the US for brewing trials; these would set the foundation for the american beer industry.
US program receives about 1000 tung seeds from Consul L. S. Wilcox and begins tung-oil culture trials that later become a southern industry
Fairchild ships seedless grapes of the Sultanina variety. While not the first introduction into the US, it was the most successful one for raisins and wine making. Other orchards were also shipped
Fairchild introduces kale forms from Central Europe
Collects and ships pistachio material (first budded pistachios to the US) plus lentils; investigates Poros seedless lemon claims
Builds a major mango collection centered on Alphonso and related elite cultivars
Attempts mangosteen collection and secures Saigon mango seed
Collects date palm offshoots and dryland staples from Mesopotamia
Receives Quetta nectarine seeds/cuttings that later prove valuable in US breeding

Crop: Wasabi; Loquat; Zoysia grass; persimmon; Udo; Bamboo
Event: Collects Japanese edible and landscape plants and builds cherry/bamboo acquisition network
July 27: Meyer departs on first China expedition with persimmon target mission
Frank Meyer discovers and sends material for what would later be known as the Meyer lemon

Crop: Soybeans; asparagus; oats; millet; wild pears; persimmons; bamboo; apricots; ornamentals
Event: Frank Meyer's first expedition ships ~20 tons of plant material; includes first formal soybean/asparagus entries through the program
Joseph Rock collected chaulmoogra seed for cultivation to stabilize medicinal oil supply used in leprosy treatment programs
Dasheen collection from Puerto Rico and Florida holdings was consolidated at Brooksville for hardiness and varietal testing

Crop: Walnuts; hazelnuts; chestnuts (blight-resistant); jujubes; pears; apples; peaches; haw
Event: Frank Meyer ships chestnut material resistant to blight and other high-value temperate crops
Wilson Popenoe collects and ships great Guatemalan avocado selections.
During Frank Meyer's final expedition, he sends ginkgo and Ichang citrus material before his tragic death.
Fairchild shpped a dwarf variery of pineapples sweeter and less fibrous. This varierty did not caught on with American farmers.
Fairchild ships back cultivars of Arracacha back to the US while on his last official plant exploring expedition.
Fairchild ships back Corozo back to the US while on his last official plant exploring expedition.
